text: The surgeon marks the site for the feeding jejunostomy on the left paramedian position above the umbilicus using a FIMM port.
The surgeon places two working ports and a camera port in the right flank, preparing for the jejunostomy procedure.
The indication for feeding jejunostomy in this patient was a post-sleeve gastrectomy leak with a bronchovisovisal fistula in addition to severe reflux symptoms.

A MAGA-S stent has been placed on the conduit to control the leak and the bronchovisovisal fistula and is planned for a feeding jejunostomy with a subsequent plan for a Rouenvai gastric bypass once the fistula heals.
A 5mm trocar is positioned at the marked site for the feeding jejunostomy in the left paramedian area above the umbilicus.
The surgeon identifies the duodenojejunal (DJ) flexure and selects the site for the feeding jejunostomy, marked in the left paramedian position above the umbilicus.
The surgeon identifies the site for the feeding jejunostomy, located fifty centimeters from the DJ flexure, with plans to convert this site for a Roux-en-Y gastric bypass after the fistula heals.

In a morbidly obese patient, the surgeon marks the site of the feeding jejunostomy (FJ) with diathermy. Four transfacial fixation sutures are placed above, below, medially, and laterally to hitch the jejunum up.
The surgeon secures the feeding jejunostomy to the parietal wall using sutures on all four sides, ensuring stable fixation of the jejunum.
The surgeon uses a suture passer to place the superior and lateral transfacial fixation sutures, securing the jejunum with a stitch using a standard needle holder.
The surgeon places a lateral hitching stitch to secure the jejunum, using a suture to fix it in position as part of the feeding jejunostomy procedure.
The surgeon lifts the jejunum using hitching sutures and creates a small enterotomy at the marked site for the feeding jejunostomy.
The surgeon places a bursting suture around the opening created for the feeding jejunostomy using three 0 Vicryl sutures, securing the jejunal tissue at the marked site.
The surgeon inserts a twelfth French tube into the jejunum to complete the feeding jejunostomy, securing the tube in place within the previously prepared opening.
The surgeon introduces the feeding jejunostomy tube through the previously marked site using a 5mm trocar, employing a Merry lens for guidance.
The surgeon uses hitching sutures to elevate the tube, facilitating the insertion of the feeding jejunostomy into the distal jejunal limb.

The surgeon introduces the feeding jejunostomy tube into the distal jejunum, advancing it approximately twelve to twenty centimeters. The position of the tube is confirmed visually, and the pearl string suture is tied to secure the tube in place.
The surgeon uses a suture passer and nail holder to place medial and inferior hitching sutures, securing the jejunum to the parietal peritoneum.

The surgeon places an additional fixing suture approximately three centimeters distal to the initial feeding jejunostomy fixation site, ensuring a two-point fixation with a broad base to prevent twisting of the tube.
The surgeon tightens the four hitching sutures around the feeding jejunostomy and the distal hitching suture for two-point fixation. The neoprotonium is partially deflated during this process.
The surgeon checks the patency of the feeding jejunostomy by instilling saline into the distal limb, observing the free flow of fluid through the tube.
The completed feeding jejunostomy is shown with a broad base of excision, designed to prevent torsion of the jejunostomy tube.
